question:
Let $f$ be a real function on the real line with continuous third
derivative.  Prove that there exists a point $a$ such that
\[f(a)\cdot f'(a) \cdot f''(a) \cdot f'''(a)\geq 0 .\]
solution:
If at least one of $f(a)$, $f'(a)$, $f''(a)$, or $f'''(a)$ vanishes
at some point $a$, then we are done.  Hence we may assume each of
$f(x)$, $f'(x)$, $f''(x)$, and $f'''(x)$ is either strictly positive
or strictly negative on the real line.  By replacing $f(x)$ by $-f(x)$
if necessary, we may assume $f''(x)>0$; by replacing $f(x)$
by $f(-x)$ if necessary, we may assume $f'''(x)>0$.  (Notice that these
substitutions do not change the sign of $f(x) f'(x) f''(x) f'''(x)$.)
Now $f''(x)>0$ implies that $f'(x)$ is increasing, and $f'''(x)>0$
implies that $f'(x)$ is convex, so that $f'(x+a)>f'(x)+a f''(x)$
for all $x$ and $a$.  By
letting $a$ increase in the latter inequality, we see that $f'(x+a)$
must be positive for sufficiently large $a$; it follows that
$f'(x)>0$
for all $x$.  Similarly, $f'(x)>0$ and $f''(x)>0$ imply
that $f(x)>0$ for all $x$.  Therefore $f(x) f'(x) f''(x) f'''(x)>0$ for
all $x$, and we are done.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $realfunc$ be a real function on the real line with continuous third
derivative.  Prove that there exists a point $specialpt$ such that
\[
realfunc(specialpt)\cdot realfunc'(specialpt) \cdot realfunc''(specialpt) \cdot realfunc'''(specialpt)\geq 0 .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
If at least one of $realfunc(specialpt)$, $realfunc'(specialpt)$, $realfunc''(specialpt)$, or $realfunc'''(specialpt)$ vanishes
at some point $specialpt$, then we are done.  Hence we may assume each of
$realfunc(realvar)$, $realfunc'(realvar)$, $realfunc''(realvar)$, and $realfunc'''(realvar)$ is either strictly positive
or strictly negative on the real line.  By replacing $realfunc(realvar)$ by $-realfunc(realvar)$
if necessary, we may assume $realfunc''(realvar)>0$; by replacing $realfunc(realvar)$
by $realfunc(-realvar)$ if necessary, we may assume $realfunc'''(realvar)>0$.  (Notice that these
substitutions do not change the sign of $realfunc(realvar) realfunc'(realvar) realfunc''(realvar) realfunc'''(realvar)$.)
Now $realfunc''(realvar)>0$ implies that $realfunc'(realvar)$ is increasing, and $realfunc'''(realvar)>0$
implies that $realfunc'(realvar)$ is convex, so that $realfunc'(realvar+specialpt)>realfunc'(realvar)+specialpt realfunc''(realvar)$
for all $realvar$ and $specialpt$.  By
letting $specialpt$ increase in the latter inequality, we see that $realfunc'(realvar+specialpt)$
must be positive for sufficiently large $specialpt$; it follows that
$realfunc'(realvar)>0$
for all $realvar$.  Similarly, $realfunc'(realvar)>0$ and $realfunc''(realvar)>0$ imply
that $realfunc(realvar)>0$ for all $realvar$.  Therefore $realfunc(realvar) realfunc'(realvar) realfunc''(realvar) realfunc'''(realvar)>0$ for
all $realvar$, and we are done.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $watering$ be a real function on the real line with continuous third
derivative.  Prove that there exists a point $sunflower$ such that
\[watering(sunflower)\cdot watering'(sunflower) \cdot watering''(sunflower) \cdot watering'''(sunflower)\geq 0 .\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
If at least one of $watering(sunflower)$, $watering'(sunflower)$, $watering''(sunflower)$, or $watering'''(sunflower)$ vanishes
at some point $sunflower$, then we are done.  Hence we may assume each of
$watering(porcelain)$, $watering'(porcelain)$, $watering''(porcelain)$, and $watering'''(porcelain)$ is either strictly positive
or strictly negative on the real line.  By replacing $watering(porcelain)$ by $-watering(porcelain)$
if necessary, we may assume $watering''(porcelain)>0$; by replacing $watering(porcelain)$
by $watering(-porcelain)$ if necessary, we may assume $watering'''(porcelain)>0$.  (Notice that these
substitutions do not change the sign of $watering(porcelain) watering'(porcelain) watering''(porcelain) watering'''(porcelain)$.)
Now $watering''(porcelain)>0$ implies that $watering'(porcelain)$ is increasing, and $watering'''(porcelain)>0$
implies that $watering'(porcelain)$ is convex, so that $watering'(porcelain+sunflower)>watering'(porcelain)+sunflower watering''(porcelain)$
for all $porcelain$ and $sunflower$.  By
letting $sunflower$ increase in the latter inequality, we see that $watering'(porcelain+sunflower)$
must be positive for sufficiently large $sunflower$; it follows that
$watering'(porcelain)>0$
for all $porcelain$.  Similarly, $watering'(porcelain)>0$ and $watering''(porcelain)>0$ imply
that $watering(porcelain)>0$ for all $porcelain$.  Therefore $watering(porcelain) watering'(porcelain) watering''(porcelain) watering'''(porcelain)>0$ for
all $porcelain$, and we are done.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $nonvarying$ be a real function on the real line with continuous third
derivative.  Prove that there exists a point $dispersion$ such that
\[nonvarying(dispersion)\cdot nonvarying'(dispersion) \cdot nonvarying''(dispersion) \cdot nonvarying'''(dispersion)\geq 0 .\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
If at least one of $nonvarying(dispersion)$, $nonvarying'(dispersion)$, $nonvarying''(dispersion)$, or $nonvarying'''(dispersion)$ vanishes
at some point $dispersion$, then we are done.  Hence we may assume each of
$nonvarying(staticvalue)$, $nonvarying'(staticvalue)$, $nonvarying''(staticvalue)$, and $nonvarying'''(staticvalue)$ is either strictly positive
or strictly negative on the real line.  By replacing $nonvarying(staticvalue)$ by $-nonvarying(staticvalue)$
if necessary, we may assume $nonvarying''(staticvalue)>0$; by replacing $nonvarying(staticvalue)$
by $nonvarying(-staticvalue)$ if necessary, we may assume $nonvarying'''(staticvalue)>0$.  (Notice that these
substitutions do not change the sign of $nonvarying(staticvalue)\, nonvarying'(staticvalue)\, nonvarying''(staticvalue)\, nonvarying'''(staticvalue)$.)
Now $nonvarying''(staticvalue)>0$ implies that $nonvarying'(staticvalue)$ is increasing, and $nonvarying'''(staticvalue)>0$
implies that $nonvarying'(staticvalue)$ is convex, so that $nonvarying'(staticvalue+dispersion)>nonvarying'(staticvalue)+dispersion\, nonvarying''(staticvalue)$
for all $staticvalue$ and $dispersion$.  By
letting $dispersion$ increase in the latter inequality, we see that $nonvarying'(staticvalue+dispersion)$
must be positive for sufficiently large $dispersion$; it follows that
$nonvarying'(staticvalue)>0$
for all $staticvalue$.  Similarly, $nonvarying'(staticvalue)>0$ and $nonvarying''(staticvalue)>0$ imply
that $nonvarying(staticvalue)>0$ for all $staticvalue$.  Therefore $nonvarying(staticvalue)\, nonvarying'(staticvalue)\, nonvarying''(staticvalue)\, nonvarying'''(staticvalue)>0$ for
all $staticvalue$, and we are done.

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp$ be a real function on the real line with continuous third
derivative.  Prove that there exists a point $kmlpqrst$ such that
\[qzxwvtnp(kmlpqrst)\cdot qzxwvtnp'(kmlpqrst) \cdot qzxwvtnp''(kmlpqrst) \cdot qzxwvtnp'''(kmlpqrst)\geq 0 .\]
garbled_string.solution:
If at least one of $qzxwvtnp(kmlpqrst)$, $qzxwvtnp'(kmlpqrst)$, $qzxwvtnp''(kmlpqrst)$, or $qzxwvtnp'''(kmlpqrst)$ vanishes
at some point $kmlpqrst$, then we are done.  Hence we may assume each of
$qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$, $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)$, $qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla)$, and $qzxwvtnp'''(hjgrksla)$ is either strictly positive
or strictly negative on the real line.  By replacing $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$ by $-qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$
if necessary, we may assume $qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla)>0$; by replacing $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$
by $qzxwvtnp(-hjgrksla)$ if necessary, we may assume $qzxwvtnp'''(hjgrksla)>0$.  (Notice that these
substitutions do not change the sign of $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp'''(hjgrksla)$.)
Now $qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla)>0$ implies that $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)$ is increasing, and $qzxwvtnp'''(hjgrksla)>0$
implies that $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)$ is convex, so that $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla+kmlpqrst)>qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)+kmlpqrst qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla)$
for all $hjgrksla$ and $kmlpqrst$.  By
letting $kmlpqrst$ increase in the latter inequality, we see that $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla+kmlpqrst)$
must be positive for sufficiently large $kmlpqrst$; it follows that
$qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)>0$
for all $hjgrksla$.  Similarly, $qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla)>0$ and $qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla)>0$ imply
that $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)>0$ for all $hjgrksla$.  Therefore $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp'(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp''(hjgrksla) qzxwvtnp'''(hjgrksla)>0$ for
all $hjgrksla$, and we are done.

kernel_variant.question:
Let f: \mathbb{R} \to  \mathbb{R} be three-times differentiable and assume that the third derivative f''' is continuous on \mathbb{R}.  Prove that there exists a point a \in  \mathbb{R} for which

          f(a)\cdot f'(a)\cdot f''(a)\cdot f'''(a) \geq  0.
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  If any of f, f', f'', f''' vanishes somewhere we are done.
---------------------------------------------------------------
Suppose there exists x with f(x)=0 or f'(x)=0 or f''(x)=0 or f'''(x)=0.  Then at this x the product f(x)f'(x)f''(x)f'''(x) is 0 \geq  0, so the assertion holds with a = x.

From now on we therefore assume that none of the four functions ever vanishes:
(1) f(x), f'(x), f''(x), f'''(x) keep a fixed (strict) sign on \mathbb{R}.

Step 2.  Normalising the signs of f'' and f''' without touching the product.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------
Two operations leave the sign of the product f f' f'' f''' unchanged:
*  replacing f by -f, which multiplies each factor by -1;
*  reflecting the graph, i.e. passing to g(x)=f(-x); then g, g'' keep the sign of f, f'' while g', g''' change sign, so again the product is unaltered.

By first multiplying by -1 we may achieve f''>0 everywhere, and if afterwards f''' is negative we additionally reflect.  Consequently we may assume once and for all that
(2) f''(x) > 0 and f'''(x) > 0 for every x\in \mathbb{R}.

Step 3.  Consequences of (2).
----------------------------
Because f''>0, the derivative f' is strictly increasing; because f'''>0, the function f'' itself is increasing.

Fix x_0 \in  \mathbb{R} and write m := f''(x_0) > 0.  For every x \geq  x_0 we have
      f'(x) = f'(x_0) + \int _{x_0}^{x} f''(t)
             \geq  f'(x_0) + m(x - x_0).                                (3)
The right-hand side is positive once x > x_0 - f'(x_0)/m.  Thus there exists a real number N such that
      f'(x) > 0  for all x \geq  N.                                    (4)
(Note that no statement is made for x < N; f' could be negative there.)

Integrating (3) once more, for x \geq  x_0 we obtain
      f(x) = f(x_0) + \int _{x_0}^{x} f'(t)
           \geq  f(x_0) + \int _{x_0}^{x} (f'(x_0) + m(t - x_0))dt
           = f(x_0) + (x - x_0)f'(x_0) + m(x - x_0)^2/2.              (5)
The quadratic term dominates for large x; hence there exists R \geq  max{x_0,N} such that
      f(x) > 0 for every x \geq  R.                                   (6)

Step 4.  Choice of the required point.
-------------------------------------
Pick any a \geq  R.  Then by (4) and (6) we have
      f(a) > 0, f'(a) > 0, and, by (2), f''(a) > 0, f'''(a) > 0.
Consequently
      f(a)\cdot f'(a)\cdot f''(a)\cdot f'''(a) > 0 \geq  0.
Thus the demanded point a exists, completing the proof.

\blacksquare

core_steps: If any of f, f', f'', f''' vanishes somewhere, the desired sign is attained immediately
Else make sign-normalizations f→±f and x→−x so that f''>0 and f''' >0 while leaving the product’s sign unchanged
With f''>0, f' is increasing; with f''' >0, f' is convex ⇒ f'(x+a) > f'(x)+a f''(x)
Choosing large a forces f'(x+a)>0; monotonicity then gives f'>0 everywhere, and f'>0 together with f''>0 yields f>0 everywhere
Thus f·f'·f''·f''' is positive (hence non-negative) everywhere, guaranteeing a point a with the required inequality

slot1.description: The domain only needs to be unbounded so that x+a is always admissible; any unbounded interval would suffice

slot2.description: Because the argument can start with −f if desired, the final inequality may be written with ≤ instead of ≥ without altering the proof

slot3.description: Continuity of the third derivative can be relaxed; the proof uses only the existence and constant sign of f'', f''' (which already give monotonicity/convexity)
